title: About
text:
Hi, I’m Ryan! I am a data scientist who specializes in transforming raw data into predictive models and actionable insights. I am pursuing my M.S. in Data Science at Drexel University, having earned my B.S. from the University of Pittsburgh in 2025.
This site collects data science projects from my undergraduate and graduate work, spanning applied ML, time series forecasting, statistical modeling, recommender systems, analytics dashboards, and data science pipelines.
Currently, my main focus is designing a custom Spotify recommendation system and analytics dashboard. This project blends advanced algorithms with user-friendly visualizations to explore listening habits and personalized recommendations. My portfolio demonstrates my ability to apply these skills across diverse industries. I have built machine learning models to categorize premium pricing tiers for the Milwaukee Bucks and engineered time series forecasts for real estate markets. I am eager to tackle complex challenges using data-driven solutions.

title: Ryan Quinlan
text:
Hi, I’m Ryan. This site collects data science projects from my undergraduate and graduate work, spanning applied machine learning tasks, time series forecasting, and statistical modeling to solve complex business problems
Browse the projects page for writeups, code, reports, and notebooks. More about me.

title: Song Popularity Machine Learning Final Project
text:
Final project for Stat 1361 — Statistical Machine Learning at the University of Pittsburgh. Given 1,200 Spotify tracks split across pop, rock, and jazz with 15 audio features per track (danceability, energy, valence, tempo, acousticness, and others), the task was to predict each track’s popularity score on a 0–100 scale.
I worked through the full modeling pipeline, exploratory data analysis, multicollinearity diagnostics, influential-point removal, variable-importance analysis, and a head-to-head comparison of eight regression and tree-based models. Then I refit the best model on the full training set to generate predictions for a 600-track test set.

I worked through the full modeling pipeline, exploratory data analysis, multicollinearity diagnostics, influential-point removal, variable-importance analysis, and a head-to-head comparison of eight regression and tree-based models. Then I refit the best model on the full training set to generate predictions for a 600-track test set.

title: Song Popularity Machine Learning Final Project
Song Popularity Machine Learning Final Project
text:
Results
Bagging won, with a test MSE of 669 — a 23% improvement over the OLS baseline (869) and the lowest of any model tested:



Model
Test MSE




Bagging (mtry = 15)
669


Boosting
828


Forward step-wise + GAM
843


LASSO
867


Linear regression
869


Ridge regression
869


Regression tree
880


Pruned regression tree
938



Three things stood out:

Genre dominated. Pop tracks had a median popularity around 65; rock and jazz both clustered near 5. Track genre was the single most important predictor by a wide margin in every variable-importance plot.
A handful of audio features did the rest. Duration, danceability, valence, and acousticness ranked next in random-forest importance. Mode, key, and time signature contributed almost nothing.
Pruning hurt. Cross-validation suggested a 5-leaf tree, but the pruned model performed worse than the unpruned tree (938 vs. 880). Variance reduction from bagging mattered more than tree-level interpretability for this problem.
